text.title: Business Process Analysis: Five-Stage Loop
text.summary: Five-stage BPA loop for documenting current-state processes, classifying activity value, and designing measurable future-state improvements.

section.title: Stage 1: Identify Processes

rule.statement: Before modeling any individual process, build a scoped inventory table with columns: process name, owner, priority, in-scope flag. Prioritize by nva_minutes_per_year and strategic fit; deep-model only the top decile.
rule.rationale: Enterprise programs routinely discover 800+ processes; without a Pareto cut, documentation effort consumes more resources than the improvements deliver. The scorecard prevents portfolio bloat.

section.reference: Ranks redesign/automation candidates from scorecard.csv by NVA x feasibility

rule.statement: Capture every workaround and exception, not just the official flow; record verbatim quotes from process participants for any anomaly in evidence/anomalies.md.
rule.rationale: Shadow steps and workarounds are the actual process; documenting only the intended flow produces a diagram nobody recognizes and analysis based on fiction. Verbatim quotes prevent agent rewrites that erase institutional knowledge.

list.item: Activities — what is done.
Actors — who does it (person, role, or system).
Inputs and outputs — data entering and leaving each step.
Decisions and business rules — gateway logic with conditions.
Systems used — applications touched at each step.
Exceptions and workarounds — deviations from the official flow.

rule.statement: Classify every step as Value-Adding (VA), Business-Necessary (BN), or Non-Value-Adding (NVA); compute NVA percentage before proposing any improvement.
rule.rationale: Redesigning before quantifying NVA produces a wish list, not a business case. The NVA percentage anchors the expected-benefit calculation in Stage 4.

list.item: Value analysis — which steps add direct customer or business value (VA), which are required but not directly valuable (BN, e.g., compliance), which are waste (NVA).
Time analysis — where are delays; compute cycle time vs. touch time ratio.
Cost analysis — cost per transaction at each step.
Quality analysis — where errors occur and their rework cost.
Pain point analysis — participant frustrations (captured from elicitation, not inferred).

rule.statement: Produce a structured diff table (step | current actor | future actor | current system | future system | change type | risk | dependency) instead of a narrative description.
rule.rationale: A narrative future state hides integration prerequisites; the diff table forces specificity and makes dependencies visible before implementation starts.

list.item: Eliminate — remove NVA steps (e.g., redundant approvals, double-entry).
Automate — replace manual steps with technology where ROI is positive.
Parallelize — run sequential steps concurrently where data dependencies allow.
Simplify — reduce decision complexity by raising thresholds or merging branches.
Integrate — connect systems to eliminate manual handoffs.
Standardize — enforce one consistent execution path to eliminate variation-driven rework.

rule.statement: Do not advance to Stage 5 without Stage 4 review by the portfolio agent (enterprise context) or process owner (project context); validation is a decision gate, not a formality.
rule.rationale: Future-state designs based on incorrect process models or unverified business rules generate implementation work in the wrong direction; the stage gate catches these before any code is written.

list.item: Review future state with process owners and affected stakeholders.
Identify system requirements generated by the redesign.
Define transition plan: how the organization moves from current to future state.
Define success metrics: quantitative targets against current baseline.

antipattern.description: Documenting the ideal process instead of the actual process.
antipattern.reason: Analysis of an ideal state produces improvements to a process nobody runs; the current-state documentation must reflect observed reality, not policy intent.
Future-state benefits have no evidence anchor without volume, cycle time, and cost baselines; the business case is undefendable.
